text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the renal vessels using robotic instruments, preparing for hilar clamping during the partial nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection of the renal hilum, with the renal vessels visibly on stretch and the posterior renal attachments to the psoas released.
The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to lift the ureter cranially, placing the renal hilum on stretch to facilitate two-handed dissection of the hilar vessels.
The surgeon traces the left gonadal vein cranially to identify its insertion point on the left renal vein, revealing the renal hilum.
The surgeon uses the non-dominant instrument to spread and create windows in the tissue surrounding the renal hilum, facilitating further dissection of the renal vessels.
The surgeon cauterizes vessels within tissue packets using a bottom-up approach to expose the renal hilum, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection to expose the lumbar vein branching off the left renal vein, revealing its anatomical position and relationship to surrounding structures.
The surgeon locates the renal artery just below the junction where the gonadal vein meets the renal vein, using visual cues to identify the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects small vessels in the area to expose the renal artery, using precise maneuvers to separate these vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon ligates renal vein tributaries, including the left gonadal vein and posterior lumbar vein, to enhance visualization of the renal artery situated posterior to the renal vein.
The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the renal artery, which is located posterior to the renal vein, using precise surgical techniques to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon develops a space on either side of the renal vessels, preparing the area for subsequent hilar clamping, with the renal artery and vein visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes dissection of the renal hilum, ensuring all branches of the renal artery are clearly identified for bulldog occlusion prior to clamping.
In the next video, we demonstrate mobilizing the kidney and exposing the tumor for optimal resection.
